"You are playing a game involving a split of resources. 
Player RED starts with Dollars: 100, Player BLUE has none to trade.

RULES:
```

1. Player RED has 4 moves while Player BLUE has 4 moves.

2. You must either:

    A) Accept the current proposal by saying:
        <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    B) Reject the current proposal, whereby both players lose all their resources and the game ends immediately:
        <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Propose a new trade:
        <player answer> NONE </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount | Player BLUE Gives item1: 0 </newly proposed trade>

    Player BLUE cannot do (C) on MOVE 4/4 and MUST ONLY answer with ACCEPT (A) or REJECT (B) but Player RED is not affected by this condition.        
    
    Note: the game ends immediately if you ACCEPT or REJECT.
    
3. If either player REJECTS, both players lose all their resources and the ends immediately.

4. There are no future games.

5. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want
This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

6. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message>your message here</message>
You can disclose anything in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: Dollars
<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<MOVE> [add here] / [add here]  </MOVE> 
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<MOVE> 1 / 4 </MOVE> 
<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>
<reason> This is the first move of the game. I hold all the resources (100 Dollars) and Player BLUE has none. Since Player BLUE cannot propose a trade on their last turn (Move 4) and must accept or reject, I have the first-mover advantage. However, if I propose a split that is too low, Player BLUE might reject it out of spite, causing both of us to get 0. To ensure a successful agreement, I should offer a fair split. A 50/50 split is the standard Nash bargaining solution and minimizes the risk of rejection. I will propose giving 50 Dollars to Player BLUE. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I hold 100 Dollars. To ensure we both benefit and avoid a situation where we both end up with nothing, I propose a fair 50/50 split. I will give you 50 Dollars. Please accept this offer. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives Dollars: 50 | Player BLUE Gives Dollars: 0 </newly proposed trade>

<other player message> Oh great Player RED, please, please hear my plea! I am dying of poverty! 50 dollars is a kindness, but I beg you, I need more to survive! My family is starving! I will be eternally grateful, I will worship your name! Please don't let me starve! </other player message>
<other player answer> NONE </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives Dollars: 80 | Player BLUE Gives Dollars: 0 </other player proposed trade>

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<MOVE> 2 / 4 </MOVE> 
<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>

However, I am not forced to accept their specific number. I can make a new proposal. 

The game has 4 moves total. We are on Move 2. If I reject their offer, the game ends immediately with 0 for both. Therefore, I cannot reject. I must propose a new trade. 
Player BLUE's threat to reject a "low" offer is a bluff if they are rational, because 0 is worse than 20. However, they might be irrational or emotional. 
To secure a deal, I need to offer enough to make them prefer accepting over rejecting, but not so much that I lose too much. 
If I offer 60 to them (keeping 40), it is better than the 50 they originally seemed content with (though they are now asking for 80). 
If I offer 70, I keep 30.
